ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Biangbiang Noodles. Closest defensible match is the generic Chinese noodle dish class FOODON_03315595. A more specific entry does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Wheat noodles, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for hand-pulled wheat noodle dish with oil-splash dressing
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Biangbiang Noodles (Shaanxi belt noodles). A00LC (wheat noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Composite dish code not available in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Biangbiang Noodles or Shaanxi belt noodles as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Biangbiang Noodles is approximately one bowl (around 400 g), comprising the wide hand-pulled noodles plus the oil-splash chili dressing with garlic and scallions.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Noodles, Chinese, chow mein, dry (SR Legacy) and Noodles, egg, cooked (SR Legacy); composite estimation for oil-dressed wheat noodle dish
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Biangbiang Noodles (Shaanxi oil-splash belt noodles). Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked wheat noodles (FDC ID 20113) and vegetable oil, adjusted for the oil-splash dressing (chili oil, garlic, scallions, soy sauce, vinegar). Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against this USDA composite and retained where consistent. Sodium reflects soy sauce contribution. Values represent a plausible per-100g profile for the prepared dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of wheat noodles — analogous estimate based on cooked white wheat noodles (GI ~53–65, medium category); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Biangbiang Noodles in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white wheat noodles (GI range 53–65 per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The thick, chewy texture and significant oil content of the oil-splash dressing are expected to moderately attenuate the glycemic response relative to thinner wheat noodles. GL per serving calculated as: (65 × 17.0 g carbohydrate per 100g × 400g serving) / 100 = 44.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~367 mg per 100g; ~1,468 mg per 400g bowl) from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200g and request reduced soy sauce, as a standard bowl can deliver approximately 1,468 mg sodium — over 60% of the recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~65) from refined wheat flour noodles; moderate carbohydrate load (~68g per 400g bowl)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 200g, pair the noodles with additional non-starchy vegetables and protein, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the medium GI and high carbohydrate load of a full bowl can cause a significant glycemic excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (~5g per 100g) from chili oil dressing; high sodium from soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the combined sodium and fat load; reducing the amount of chili oil used in the dressing and limiting portion size to 200–250g can help keep saturated fat and sodium within heart-healthy targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Biangbiang Noodles (𰻞𰻞面 / 𰻞𰻞麵; also known as Biángbiángmiàn or Yóupō chěmiàn — oil-splash pulled noodles) are a celebrated specialty of Shaanxi Province in Northwestern China, particularly associated with the ancient city of Xi'an. The dish is named after the loud 'biang biang' sound produced when the dough is slapped against the work surface during hand-pulling. The noodles are extraordinarily wide — often 5–10 cm across and up to 60 cm long — resembling a leather belt, which gives rise to their colloquial name 'belt noodles'. The dough is made from wheat flour, water, and salt, then stretched and slapped by hand into a single long, thick, chewy ribbon. The most traditional preparation is the 'Youzhu' (oil-splash) style: boiled noodles are placed in a bowl, topped with a generous mound of dried chili flakes, minced garlic, and sliced scallions, then finished with a dramatic pour of smoking-hot oil that sizzles and blooms the aromatics. Soy sauce and Shaanxi black vinegar are typically added for depth and acidity. The result is a dish that is intensely fragrant, spicy, savory, and deeply satisfying, with a thick chewy texture that distinguishes it from all other Chinese noodle styles. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides approximately 125 kcal, 3.8g protein, 5.0g fat, and 17.0g carbohydrate, with notable sodium (~367mg) from the soy sauce dressing. The dish is vegetarian and vegan in its traditional oil-splash form. Clinically, the high sodium content is a concern for hypertension, the medium glycemic index (~65) and moderate carbohydrate load require portion control for Type 2 Diabetes management, and the combined sodium and fat load warrants caution for cardiovascular disease. The dish is also famous for its name character — one of the most complex Chinese characters ever written, requiring 57 strokes — making it a cultural icon as much as a culinary one.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Biangbiang Noodles exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for cooked wheat noodles and vegetable oil, cross-checked against the AI-generated input values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 for cooked white wheat noodles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT SOURCE: No approved primary source contains an exact entry for Biangbiang Noodles. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked wheat noodles, FDC ID 20113) adjusted for the oil-splash dressing. Input AI-generated values were cross-checked and adjusted where inconsistent with the USDA composite. Protein was revised downward from 7.5g to 3.8g per 100g to align with USDA cooked wheat noodle data (~3.8g/100g for cooked noodles with oil dressing); the input value of 7.5g appeared inconsistent with a predominantly carbohydrate-based noodle dish. Carbohydrate was revised upward from 12.5g to 17.0g per 100g to better reflect the wheat noodle base. Saturated fat was revised from 1.67g to 0.7g to reflect predominantly plant-oil dressing. Cholesterol revised to 0.0 (traditional vegan preparation contains no animal products). Vitamin A revised to 6.0 mcg RAE (chili and scallion contribution). Vitamin B12 and Vitamin D set to 0.0 as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 2. ZERO VALUES: cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — traditional oil-splash Biangbiang Noodles contain no animal products); vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — vegan plant-based dish); vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal-derived or fortified ingredients in traditional preparation). Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed negligible — plant oil dressing, no hydrogenated fats). 3. NEGATIVE SENTINEL: water_g is set to -1 as moisture content for the prepared dish is not reported in USDA FoodData Central for this composite preparation. 4. GI: GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white wheat noodles per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 400g serving = (65 × 17.0 × 400) / 10000 = 44.2. 5. PORTIONS: Input contained two duplicate '1 serving (350g)' entries and one '1 bowl (600g)' entry from grabfood (a forbidden source). The default serving has been set to 400g (1 bowl) as a defensible estimate for a restaurant portion, with the grabfood source not used as primary evidence. 6. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn URI FOODON_03315595 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are approximate matches only; no exact ontology code exists for Biangbiang Noodles. 7. MULTILINGUAL: This is a Chinese / Shaanxi dish, not a Singaporean dish; therefore Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not required and are set to null. Chinese simplified and traditional forms are provided.
